sections.heading: Why Time Management Matters for Developers
sections.body: Developers who manage time well ship more code, make fewer mistakes, and experience less burnout. The difference between a junior and senior developer isn't just skill — it's how they allocate their attention across competing priorities.

sections.heading: The Eisenhower Matrix
sections.body: Dwight Eisenhower used a simple 2x2 grid to decide what deserves your time. Split tasks by urgency and importance into four quadrants:

code.snippet:
URGENT              NOT URGENT
              ┌─────────────────┬─────────────────┐
  IMPORTANT   │   DO FIRST       │   SCHEDULE       │
              │   Deadlines      │   Deep work      │
              │   Production bugs│   Learning       │
              │   Client calls   │   Architecture   │
              ├─────────────────┼─────────────────┤
  NOT         │   DELEGATE       │   ELIMINATE      │
  IMPORTANT   │   Status meetings│   Scrolling      │
              │   Some emails    │   Busywork       │
              │   Admin tasks    │   Perfectionism  │
              └─────────────────┴─────────────────┘
code.explanation: Quadrant 1 (urgent + important): Handle immediately. Quadrant 2 (not urgent + important): This is where your career grows — schedule dedicated time. Quadrant 3 (urgent + not important): Delegate if possible. Quadrant 4 (neither): Eliminate ruthlessly.

sections.heading: Time Blocking: Protect Your Calendar
sections.body: Time blocking means assigning every hour of your workday to a specific task or category. Instead of reacting to whatever comes up, you decide in advance what gets your attention. Block 2-4 hour chunks for deep coding work, and batch all meetings into a single afternoon slot. Google Calendar and Outlook both support recurring blocks — set them once and protect them weekly.

jargon.definition: A decision framework that categorizes tasks by urgency and importance into four quadrants, helping you prioritize what actually matters.
A scheduling method where you assign specific time periods to specific tasks, rather than working from a to-do list reactively.
Cognitive-intensive work performed in a state of distraction-free concentration, typically lasting 2-4 hours.
Urgent tasks demand immediate attention; important tasks contribute to long-term goals. Most people confuse the two.

summary: Use the Eisenhower Matrix to classify tasks by urgency and importance. Time block your calendar to protect deep work sessions from interruptions. Most developers spend too much time in Quadrant 1 (urgent + important) and not enough in Quadrant 2 (important but not urgent), which is where career growth happens.
